intro: Hey, welcome back to **The Weekly Rundown** — grab a snack, get comfortable, and maybe say a little prayer for Eric Weddle's knees while you're at it. It's been one of *those* weeks in sports, from Monaco to the French Open to a Knicks fan in New York who definitely did not calm down and we would not have it any other way.

stories.headline: Eric Weddle's Body Is Still Sending Him the Bill From That 2022 Comeback
stories.body: Remember when Eric Weddle came out of retirement to play in the playoffs for the Rams and everyone thought it was the coolest thing ever? Well, years later, Weddle says he's still physically feeling the effects — and he wants Aaron Donald to take notes before even thinking about lacing them back up. Consider it a cautionary tale from one legend to another.

stories.headline: Kyler Murray to the Vikings? OTAs Are Already Pointing to a Frontrunner
stories.body: Things are moving fast in Minnesota, where Kyler Murray appears to be separating himself from the pack at quarterback during OTAs. It's early, sure, but when one guy is already standing out this clearly in offseason workouts, the writing tends to stay on the wall. Vikings fans have been starved for a real QB answer — this one's worth watching closely.

stories.headline: The Browns Get Primetime Love — But Only in the Preseason, Lol
stories.body: Cleveland got some scheduling news this week, and in classic Browns fashion, it comes with a catch — they're getting primetime exposure in the preseason, not the regular season. It's the kind of thing that feels like a participation trophy, but hey, a spotlight is a spotlight. Maybe they'll use it to prove the league wrong before the real games even start.

stories.headline: Spike Lee Is Absolutely Not Letting the Knicks' Finals Moment Pass Without a Flex
stories.body: New York's most famous superfan is pulling up to Madison Square Garden for Game 3 of the NBA Finals in a pair of Air Jordan 3 PEs, and honestly, what else would you expect? The Knicks are in the Finals, Spike is in rare form, and the fits are going to be must-watch television alongside the actual basketball. MSG hasn't had energy like this in a long, long time.

stories.headline: Dominick Barlow Locked In a Real Contract With Philly — Now Comes the Hard Part
stories.body: Barlow did enough in year one to earn himself a standard NBA deal with the Sixers, which is no small thing. But the scouting report has a glaring hole in it, and teams at this level will absolutely exploit it if he doesn't put in the work this offseason. Year two is where we find out if he's a rotation piece or a cautionary tale.

stories.headline: Jordan Hicks Might Be Too Expensive to Keep and Too Bad to Play
stories.body: The White Sox could be on the verge of cutting ties with reliever Jordan Hicks, who's been struggling this season and is currently on the IL. Paying $8 million for a reliever is already a tough pill to swallow — paying $8 million for a bad reliever is a whole different kind of pain. Chicago's had a rough go of it lately, so don't be shocked if Hicks gets the DFA stamp when he's cleared to return.

stories.headline: The Cubs Want Tarik Skubal, and Detroit Might Actually Listen
stories.body: ESPN floated a Cubs-Tigers trade centered around ace Tarik Skubal, and apparently it's not completely crazy — sources suggest it's a deal Detroit could at least consider. Skubal has been one of the best pitchers in baseball, so if Chicago is serious, they'd better be ready to empty the farm. This one's worth watching as the trade deadline creeps closer.

stories.headline: Soroka Saves the D-backs From an Ugly Sunday
stories.body: Michael Soroka delivered another quietly impressive outing, helping Arizona beat Washington 5-1 and avoid getting swept at home. Nobody's putting Soroka on a postseason billboard yet, but he keeps showing up when the team needs him most. The D-backs escaped the homestand with their dignity intact — and sometimes that's enough.

stories.headline: Longhorns and Ducks Bring the Heat to Austin's Super Regional Showdown
stories.body: Texas and Oregon square off in the NCAA Baseball Super Regionals tonight on ESPN, with the Longhorns playing in their own backyard at UFCU Disch-Falk Field — which is about as home-field-advantage as it gets. If you're a college baseball fan and you're not watching this, what are you even doing with your evening?

stories.headline: Kentucky Just Landed Its Most Hyped Transfer and He's Already in the Building
stories.body: Milan Momcilovic pulled out of the NBA Draft, signed with Kentucky, and apparently the whole city of Lexington collectively lost its mind — in the best way. The Iowa State transfer is expected to bring serious scoring punch to a Wildcats squad that's already licking its chops heading into next season.

stories.headline: AJ Ferrari Wants to Wrestle Alongside His Brother — and That's Actually Pretty Wholesome
stories.body: Former NCAA wrestling champion AJ Ferrari entered the transfer portal and is now saying his top priority is landing at the same school as his brother for his final college season. It's a sweet storyline in a sport that doesn't get nearly enough attention, and whatever program pulls this off is getting a serious weapon on the mat.

stories.headline: Eriksen Collapses Again — But He's Okay, And We Can Breathe
stories.body: Christian Eriksen gave everyone a serious case of déjà vu when he collapsed during Denmark's friendly against Ukraine on June 7 — five years almost to the day after his terrifying cardiac arrest at Euro 2020. The good news: he was conscious and reportedly feeling okay given the circumstances. This guy has already cheated death once, so here's hoping this was just a scare and nothing more serious.

stories.headline: World Cup vs. Olympics: The Age-Old Debate Gets a Utah Twist
stories.body: With Utah gearing up to host the 2034 Winter Olympics, locals are apparently weighing in on whether the World Cup — also coming to the US — is a bigger deal than the Games. Spoiler: soccer fans will tell you it's not even close, while everyone else will disagree loudly. It's the kind of debate that has no right answer but makes for a great argument at the pub.

stories.headline: Antonelli Owns Monaco — But Did He Lock Up the Title Too?
stories.body: Kimi Antonelli rolled into Monte Carlo already sitting pretty at the top of the drivers' standings, and the Monaco Grand Prix was his chance to really put some distance between himself and the rest of the field. Whether he converted that opportunity is the big talking point this week — and if he did, the championship picture is starting to look less like a race and more like a coronation. Keep an eye on that gap heading into the next round.

stories.headline: Former College Kid Shnaider Stuns Sabalenka at the French Open
stories.body: Diana Shnaider, who was playing college tennis at NC State not that long ago, just knocked out world number one Aryna Sabalenka at Roland Garros — and the tennis world is doing a double take. If she goes all the way, she'd be the first ex-college player to win a women's Grand Slam singles title since 1979, which is the kind of trivia that makes you stop and think about how wild that gap is. College tennis recruiting coordinators everywhere are printing this out and taping it to their office walls.

stories.headline: Off the Court Drama: Ukrainian Player Calls for Sanctions Against Shnaider
stories.body: Just as Shnaider's run is making headlines for all the right reasons, there's a serious controversy brewing around her. Ukrainian player Oleksandra Oliynykova is publicly urging tennis authorities to sanction Shnaider, alleging she lent her name and presence to a Gazprom-funded event — a Russian state energy company deeply tied to the war effort. It's a reminder that for players from Russia and Ukraine, every tournament exists against the backdrop of a very real and ongoing conflict.

stories.headline: The French Open Men's Final Is On — Here's How to Catch It
stories.body: The French Open Men's Final is set for June 7th, so clear your Sunday schedule and find a stream. Check your local listings because coverage is spread across a few channels and platforms depending on your provider. Clay court tennis at its finest — honestly one of the best annual appointments in all of sports.

stories.headline: Dylan Larkin Wants Out of Detroit, and Apparently He's Got Options
stories.body: Larkin requested a trade from the Red Wings last week, and now three teams are reportedly circling. Detroit fans have been through enough rebuilding cycles to know this stings, but if the relationship is done, it's done — now it's about getting the best return possible.

stories.headline: Milan Lucic Hangs 'Em Up After 17 Years of Making People Regret Skating Near Him
stories.body: The veteran power forward officially called it a career, closing the book on 17 NHL seasons that included a Stanley Cup with Boston in 2011. His wife Brittany shared some genuinely touching tributes online, and honestly, the outpouring of love is well-deserved — Lucic was one of the last true old-school enforcers the league will see for a while.

stories.headline: Larkin Drama Aside, Let's Talk About the Avalanche Falling Apart in the WCF
stories.body: Colorado got bounced from the Western Conference Final, and people are asking serious questions about what went wrong for a team that looked like a contender. Meanwhile, the Hurricanes are dealing with their own headaches in goal, so the playoff chaos is very much still alive.

stories.headline: Nelly Korda Wins the US Women's Open Without Actually Playing That Well — And That's Kind of the Point
stories.body: Korda missed 30 greens in regulation at Riviera and still walked away with the U.S. Women's Open trophy, which tells you everything about how mentally locked in she is right now. This wasn't just a win — it's a coronation. The conversation about the face of American women's golf for the next decade starts and ends with her.

stories.headline: JT Poston Gets His Moment at the Memorial
stories.body: While Korda was stealing the weekend's headlines, J.T. Poston quietly went out and won the Memorial Tournament in a playoff. Poston is one of those guys who doesn't get nearly enough attention for how consistently solid he is, so it's nice to see him break through on a big stage.

stories.headline: Six Guys Miss a US Open Qualifier Because of a Medical Emergency at 30,000 Feet
stories.body: A group of PGA Tour Americas players missed their final U.S. Open qualifier after a medical emergency forced their flight from Mexico to divert. If that wasn't rough enough, one player — Chris Nido — did make it to the tee but had to play with a friend's clubs after leaving his own behind. Brutal doesn't even begin to cover it.
